review_summary: This unit supports derivation-focused diagnostic questions about the word2vec gradient, chain rule, and observed-minus-expected interpretation.

question_bank.question: When differentiating the log-softmax numerator with respect to the center vector, why does the observed outside vector appear?
choices: The log and exponential cancel, leaving the dot product whose derivative is the outside vector.
The denominator disappears because all vocabulary probabilities are ignored.
The derivative of every dot product is always zero.
The center vector is treated as a constant that cannot be updated.
question_bank.explanation: The numerator is the score for the observed outside word. After simplifying log of an exponential, differentiating the dot product with respect to the center vector yields that outside-word vector.

question_bank.question: What makes the denominator derivative in the softmax objective more involved than the numerator derivative?
choices: It contains a log of a sum of exponentials, so the chain rule must be applied through multiple nested functions.
It contains no parameters, so it can be ignored during training.
It is a single observed outside vector with no vocabulary terms.
It is always exactly zero when the center vector is nonzero.
question_bank.explanation: The normalization term depends on scores for all possible outside words. Differentiating it requires propagating through the outer log, the sum, and the exponentials.

question_bank.question: What does the “expected” part of the observed-minus-expected gradient represent?
choices: A softmax-weighted average of outside-word vectors under the model’s current predictions.
The one-hot identity vector for the center word.
The manually labeled definition of the observed word.
The random initialization vector before any training.
question_bank.explanation: The expectation is computed using the model’s current probability distribution over vocabulary items. It averages outside vectors according to those predicted probabilities.

question_bank.question: Why is the observed-minus-expected form a useful way to interpret the word2vec gradient?
choices: It shows updates move predictions toward the observed context and away from the model’s current mistaken expectation.
It shows gradients depend only on word spelling and never on model probabilities.
It proves that the denominator should be dropped from the objective.
It means no learning happens unless every vocabulary word is observed equally often.
question_bank.explanation: The observed term pulls the center vector toward the actual outside word, while the expected term corrects for what the model already predicted across the vocabulary.

question_bank.question: What does it mean if the observed and expected vectors match for a particular gradient direction?
choices: The derivative in that direction is zero, so the objective has no local reason to change that parameter direction.
The model must restart from random vectors.
The softmax distribution is invalid because probabilities no longer sum to one.
The outside word has been removed from the vocabulary.
question_bank.explanation: A zero difference means the model’s expected prediction already matches the observed signal for that direction. With no discrepancy, the derivative gives no update pressure there.
